# NumReactor — Post-Launch Review

*Generated by a structured multi-pass review (6 parallel code-quality passes, 4 independent bug-hunt passes with adversarial two-verifier confirmation, 3 game-feel assessments, and a mode-ideation pass). All file/line references were re-verified against the actual source.*

## Phase 0 — Orientation

NumReactor is a SwiftUI + SpriteKit iOS arcade game with a clean, deliberate split between logic and rendering: `GameEngine.swift` is pure, dependency-free Swift holding all rules (stone/target generation, scoring, combo, heat, a 4-tier difficulty curve), `GameState.swift` is the sole `@MainActor` `ObservableObject` bridge that owns a `GameEngine` and republishes its state every tick, and `ReactorGameScene.swift` (an `SKScene`) is purely presentational, reading `GameState` each frame and forwarding taps back into it. "Rounds" aren't discrete levels — difficulty ramps continuously via `DifficultyConfig.forReactionCount(reactionCount)`, and the whole loop runs until a 0–100 "heat" meter maxes out. Data flows one way per frame (`ReactorGameScene.update` → `GameState.tick` → `GameEngine` → republish) and the other way on input (SpriteKit hit-test → `GameState.selectStone` → `GameEngine`). This is a good foundation — the logic/rendering boundary is real and testable — but as detailed below, the boundary has started to blur in a few places, and `ReactorGameScene.swift` (1,186 lines) is already carrying more responsibility than one file should.

---

## Phase 1 — Code Quality & Architecture

Findings below are grouped by severity. 31 raw findings were collected across 6 independent passes (core engine, state bridge, SpriteKit scene, SwiftUI layer, visual theme, and a repo-wide hygiene grep); duplicates found by more than one pass are merged.

### Critical

| # | Finding | File:Line |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **`applyDecoys` can silently mint a second valid solution.** `needed = target - stones[anchorIndex].value + jitter` with `jitter = rollInt(-2...2)` — when jitter rolls exactly `0` (1-in-5 chance every time a decoy fires), the "decoy" stone's value plus its anchor sum to *exactly* `target`, i.e. it's a second, fully valid subset-sum answer, not a near-miss. This directly contradicts the documented design intent and is untested (`GameEngineTests` only checks *a* solution exists via `hasSolution()`, never that there's exactly one). Also independently surfaced as a confirmed exploit in Phase 2 below. | [GameEngine.swift:287-303](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L287-L303) |

This is listed once here as an architecture/correctness finding; see Phase 2, Bug #3, for the verified gameplay consequence and fix.

### Should-fix

| # | Finding | File:Line |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | `regenerateTarget`'s guaranteed fallback (hit when `tryPickTarget` fails all 5 retries) skips the zero-sum and single-stone-value checks that the normal path enforces, so it can hand out degenerate targets with zero signal that a degraded path fired. | [GameEngine.swift:232-249](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L232-L249) |
| 3 | `refillStones` only ever *grows* the available-slot range; it has no logic for `newCapacity < ringCapacity`. Safe today only because all 4 difficulty tiers happen to have non-decreasing `activeStoneCount` — see Phase 2, Bug #1, for the case where this assumption is actually already false in phase 4. | [GameEngine.swift:213-228](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L213-L228) |
| 4 | `allowNegatives` and `minValue` are independently-settable fields with an unenforced coupling: `rollInt(minValue...(-1))` traps at runtime if `allowNegatives == true` but `minValue > -1`. Only correct today because all 4 hand-written tiers happen to pair them right. | [GameEngine.swift:194-199](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L194-L199), [DifficultyConfig.swift](NumReactor/DifficultyConfig.swift) |
| 5 | `sync()`'s manual diff (9 `if x != engine.x { x = engine.x }` checks) requires 3 separate edit sites (property, init, diff line) for every new engine field, with no compiler link between them. A forgotten diff line means the UI silently shows stale data with no test to catch it. | [GameState.swift:53-63](NumReactor/GameState.swift#L53-L63) |
| 6 | `ReactorGameScene` is a 1,186-line god object conflating texture baking, node-tree construction, per-frame layout, touch hit-testing, and a ~340-line explosion choreography, all sharing private mutable state (`flashIntensity`, `shakeAmplitude`, `orbitPull`) with no internal seams. This is the file every future feature (new stone types, the already-built-but-unused win sequence) will have to touch. | [ReactorGameScene.swift:1-1186](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift) |
| 7 | Explosion timing is duplicated magic numbers: `0.34` (implosion duration) appears 5 times, and `spawnSecondaryPop`'s offsets are computed as `0.52 - 0.34`, `0.78 - 0.34`, `1.05 - 0.34` — arithmetic that only makes sense if you already know `detonate()` starts at elapsed 0.34. A future timing tweak requires editing 6+ call sites in lockstep. | [ReactorGameScene.swift:879-891, 959-961](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L879-L891) |
| 8 | The theme palette exists in **three independent copies**: `ForgeTheme.swift` (source of truth), `GameView.swift`/`GameOverView.swift` (hand-typed RGB literals that duplicate `ForgeTheme.accent`/`meltdownAccentText`/etc. — e.g. `Color(red: 62/255, green: 166/255, blue: 255/255)` appears 5 times in `GameView.swift` alone), and `HexChamberBackgroundView.swift` (its own private RGB struct + `mix()` function + a line-for-line reimplementation of `ForgeTheme.coreColor(danger:)`). A future palette tweak has to be made in up to 3 places and will silently drift if one is missed. | [GameView.swift:106,173-206](NumReactor/GameView.swift#L106), [GameOverView.swift:16-18](NumReactor/GameOverView.swift#L16-L18), [HexChamberBackgroundView.swift:23-40,217-222](NumReactor/HexChamberBackgroundView.swift#L23-L40) |
| 9 | Every restart tears down and rebuilds the *entire* `ReactorGameScene` from scratch (texture baking, full node tree, `SpriteView` remount via `.id(ObjectIdentifier(scene))`), rather than resetting state on the existing scene — unnecessary CPU/GPU churn on the most frequently-hit path in the game (losing → retrying). | [GameView.swift:20-21,71-79](NumReactor/GameView.swift#L20-L21) |
| 10 | `ForgeParticleSystem`'s `maxParticles = 600` cap is only checked in one internal spawn path; none of the 6 public spawn entry points (`spawnRing`, `spawnSpark`, `spawnTapEffect`, `burstShards` with count 46, etc.) check it, so overlapping bursts (rapid taps + the death sequence) can blow past the cap the code clearly intended to enforce. | [ForgeParticles.swift:57,87,188-243](NumReactor/ForgeParticles.swift#L57) |
| 11 | The stone array is republished (and diffed at `O(n)` every frame) purely to feed `GameState.stones`, but grepping the whole SwiftUI layer shows **zero views actually read it** — the only consumer, `ReactorGameScene`, reads the engine imperatively and doesn't observe Combine at all. This fires `objectWillChange` 60×/sec for no subscriber, cascading a re-diff to every view holding `gameState`. | [GameState.swift:59](NumReactor/GameState.swift#L59) |
| 12 | The 1.3s "how long after death can you retry" contract is split across two unlabeled literals in two files (`1.15` in the scene, `0.15` in the view) with no shared constant — changing one without the other silently desyncs the retry gate from the visual. | [ReactorGameScene.swift:891](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L891), [GameView.swift:64-68](NumReactor/GameView.swift#L64-L68) |
| 13 | `ContainmentOverlay` re-declares its own `ember`/`emberScore`/`accent` statics instead of reusing `ForgeTheme`, despite the hex values matching exactly. | [GameOverView.swift:16-18](NumReactor/GameOverView.swift#L16-L18) |

### Nice-to-have

- Retry-loop bounds (`0..<5`, `0..<40`) and the decoy jitter range (`-2...2`) are magic numbers living outside the file's own `// MARK: Tunables` block. [GameEngine.swift:233,245,257,296](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L233)
- `GameEngine` (334 lines) already mixes generation, scoring, and difficulty selection — fine today, worth splitting into a composed `TargetGenerator` if any one concern grows further.
- `"NumReactor.bestScore"` UserDefaults key has no single source of truth / injectable store — fine for one key, will need one if a second persisted value shows up.
- `MainMenuView`/`GameView` use two different idioms (binding vs. closure) for the same "leave this screen" action — harmless at 2 screens, will need standardizing at 3.
- Particle-burst parameter blocks (angle/speed/color/count) are hand-rolled 8+ times in `ReactorGameScene.swift` with no shared helper (821-828, 906-949, 1076-1088).
- `updateBeams` rebuilds a 21-point wavy `CGPath` from scratch every frame per selected stone via a `while` loop — the one clearly per-frame-allocating hot path in the file. [ReactorGameScene.swift:748-792](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L748-L792)
- Several `.randomElement()!` force-unwraps on explosion color arrays — safe today only because the arrays are hardcoded literals.
- `"ChakraPetch-Bold"`/`"ChakraPetch-SemiBold"` appear as raw string literals ~25 times across 3 files with no compile-time check against typos.
- `GameEngine.ReactionResult` carries `speedBonus`/`chainBonus`/`heatRiskBonus`/etc. — 8 of its 10 fields are never read by any UI code. Either wire them up (see Phase 3) or trim them.
- `HexChamberBackgroundView`'s `CADisplayLink` keeps ticking (with real per-frame trig work) even while fully obscured by the game scene; it's only torn down on window detach, not on visibility.
- `DifficultyConfig.forReactionCount`'s tier boundaries (`0..<9`, `9..<21`, `21..<41`) are bare literals in a `switch`, not a data table — expect to touch this a lot post-launch based on analytics.
- Two timer-driven `@State` flags (`comboPop`, `retryArmed`) use uncancelled `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter` — safe under SwiftUI's `@State` no-op-after-teardown behavior today, but an uncancellable pattern worth centralizing if it's copy-pasted again.

---

## Phase 2 — Bug Hunt

Every bug below was found by one hunting pass and then independently re-traced by two separate adversarial verifiers against the actual source before being included; two of the four were converged on independently by *two different* hunting passes, which is a strong signal they're real. All are reproducible through normal play, not contrived edge cases.

### Bug 1 — Ring-capacity shrink+grow corrupts stone slots in late-game (Phase 4)

**File:** [GameEngine.swift:213-228](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L213-L228) (`refillStones`)
**Confidence:** High (independently confirmed by 2 verifiers)

**Trigger:** `DifficultyConfig`'s phase-4 tier (`reactionCount >= 41`) sets `activeStoneCount` to alternate `10`/`9` by parity of `reactionCount` — confirmed at [DifficultyConfig.swift:58-59](NumReactor/DifficultyConfig.swift#L58-L59) (`count.isMultiple(of: 2) ? 10 : 9`). Every single reaction in phase 4 is therefore a capacity *shrink or grow* relative to the last. `refillStones` only appends new slots when `newCapacity > ringCapacity` (line 219-221) — on a shrink, it does nothing beyond re-offering the just-freed slot, permanently forgetting any index that was implicitly vacated by the shrink itself. On the next grow, it blindly re-donates the range `[oldRingCapacity..<newCapacity)`, which can include an index that's still occupied by a stone that survived the shrink.

**Effect:** After a few shrink/grow cycles (which happen on essentially every reaction once you're 41+ correct answers in — a real outcome for any engaged player), you get a permanently-stranded gap index (fewer visually distinct ring positions than `activeStoneCount`) *and* two live `Stone` values sharing one `ringIndex`. Since `slotBaseAngle(ringIndex) = (ringIndex/ringCapacity) × 2π` drives render position directly ([GameEngine.swift:189-192](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L189-L192)), the duplicate stones render at the exact same angle — visually overlapping, with one effectively untappable.

**Fix:** Stop inferring occupancy from a capacity integer. Track it explicitly, e.g. `let occupied = Set(stones.map(\.ringIndex))`, and compute `availableSlots = freedSlots + (0..<newCapacity).filter { !occupied.contains($0) }` unconditionally (not gated on grow vs. shrink), so no index can be donated while still occupied and none is permanently stranded.

### Bug 2 — Fallback target generation can produce `target == 0`, enabling a free-reaction exploit

**File:** [GameEngine.swift:127, 244-249](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L244-L249)
**Confidence:** High (converged on independently by 2 separate hunting passes; 3 of 4 verifier votes CONFIRMED, 1 PLAUSIBLE)

**Trigger:** `tryPickTarget()` explicitly rejects a sum of `0` (line 262). Its fallback, hit when all 5 outer regeneration attempts fail (realistically reachable in phase 3/4 once negative stones exist and the solution-length window is narrow), has **no such guard**: `target = stones.prefix(min(2, stones.count)).reduce(0) { $0 + $1.value }`. If the first two stones in the array happen to be numeric opposites (e.g. `+7` and `-7` — common once `allowNegatives` is true), `target` becomes `0`.

**Effect:** `resolveIfCorrect`'s only guard is `guard !stones.isEmpty, selectedSum == target` ([GameEngine.swift:127](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L127)) — it never checks that anything is actually *selected*. Since no stone value is ever `0`, tapping any one stone (select, sum ≠ 0) and tapping it again (deselect, sum returns to `0`) satisfies `selectedSum == target` with an **empty** selection. `resolveIfCorrect` fires as a full "correct reaction": combo increments, `maxCombo` updates, heat is relieved by a flat 12, `reactionCount` advances, and a new target/stones are generated — all for tapping one stone twice with nothing actually solved. It's repeatable indefinitely and trivially discoverable once a player notices "0" on the target readout.

**Fix:** Two independent, complementary fixes — do both: (1) mirror `tryPickTarget`'s zero-check in the fallback so `target` can never come out `0`; (2) defensively require a non-empty selection in `resolveIfCorrect`'s guard (`guard !stones.isEmpty, !usedStoneIDs.isEmpty, selectedSum == target`), which closes this exploit class regardless of how `target` ever reaches `0`.

### Bug 3 — Decoy jitter of `0` creates an unintended second valid solution

**File:** [GameEngine.swift:287-303](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L287-L303) (`applyDecoys`)
**Confidence:** High (independently confirmed by 2 verifiers; also flagged in Phase 1 as a Critical architecture finding)

**Trigger:** Any time a decoy is applied (a 20%–70% chance per resolution depending on phase) and `rollInt(-2...2)` returns exactly `0` — a 1-in-5 chance conditional on decoys firing at all.

**Effect:** `needed = target - stones[anchorIndex].value + jitter` becomes exactly `target - anchor.value`. The only guard present, `guard clamped != 0` (line 299), checks that the decoy's *own* value isn't zero — it does nothing to stop `decoy.value + anchor.value == target`. This silently defeats the entire purpose of the decoy system (the CLAUDE.md-documented intent is "near-misses," not alternate solutions) roughly 1 in 5 times a decoy fires — meaning a meaningful fraction of "hard" phase-3/4 rounds (`decoyStrength` 0.5–0.7, `solutionLengthRange` demanding 3–4 stones) are trivially solvable via an accidental 2-stone pair instead.

**Fix:** Exclude `0` from the jitter roll (pick from `[-2,-1,1,2]`, or reroll on `0`), or add an explicit post-clamp check: `guard clamped + stones[anchorIndex].value != target else { return }`. Worth a regression test that counts subset-sum solutions after decoy application and asserts it stays at exactly 1.

### Bug 4 — Rapid taps can silently drop the VFX/haptic/score-popup for a real, counted solve

**File:** [GameState.swift:44](NumReactor/GameState.swift#L44), interacting with [ReactorGameScene.swift:456-459](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L456-L459)
**Confidence:** High (converged on independently by 2 separate hunting passes, 4/4 verifier votes CONFIRMED)

**Trigger:** `GameState.selectStone` unconditionally does `lastReaction = engine.selectStone(id: id)` on every tap. `ReactorGameScene.touchesBegan` calls this synchronously from UIKit touch delivery, which is **not** frame-locked to SpriteKit's render loop — two taps can land on the main thread between two `update(_:)` calls (an ordinary occurrence during fast/flicked tapping). If tap 1 completes a correct sum (`lastReaction` set to a real `ReactionResult`) and tap 2 — landing before the next `update(_:)` — doesn't complete a sum (returns `nil`), the second assignment silently overwrites the first.

**Effect:** `ReactorGameScene.update(_:)` only reads `gameState.lastReaction` once per frame and nils it after. Because it was already clobbered, `handleReaction(_:)` never runs for the first, genuinely correct solve: no fly-into-core animation, no particle rings/sparks, no floating score text, no success haptic — even though the engine already applied the score/combo/heat/target change (visible on the very next HUD sync). The consumed stone's node is only removed via the generic non-animated reconciliation in `updateStones`. The player experiences a stone silently vanishing and the target changing with zero payoff for a real win, purely as a function of tapping quickly — exactly the play style a combo/heat-pressure game encourages.

**Fix:** Stop overwriting unconditionally. Either (a) only assign when non-nil — `if let r = engine.selectStone(id: id) { lastReaction = r }` — the minimal one-line fix, or (b) buffer reactions in an array that `GameState` appends to and `ReactorGameScene` fully drains each frame, which is more robust if 3+ taps can ever land in one window.

---

## Phase 3 — Game Feel & Playfulness

### Feedback loops (per-action)

| Action | Current feedback | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Tap/select a stone | Particle spark burst (`spawnTapEffect`, only on `!wasSelected`) | Visual only — **no haptic on the single most frequent action in the game** |
| Deselect a stone | Instant hard-cut texture swap, no particle, no haptic | Weak/absent |
| Correct reaction | 3 expanding rings + 28 sparks, fly-into-core tween, floating score text, screen flash, camera shake, `.success` haptic | Strong — the best-built moment in the game |
| Wrong/overshoot | **Nothing.** `applyOvershootPenaltyIfNeeded` returns `Void`; the only signal is the result-capsule text turning red in the HUD | Absent — confirmed no ReactionResult-equivalent, no haptic, no shake, no sound path exists for this at all |
| Combo increment | HUD number does a spring "pop" (`scaleEffect` 1→1.5→1) | Present but SwiftUI-only, no SpriteKit/haptic/audio counterpart |
| Combo break (reset to 1) | **Identical** pop animation as an increment — a combo breaking from x8 looks the same as x1→x2 | A meaningful loss currently reads as indistinguishable from a small gain |
| Difficulty tier-up | Nothing — `GameState` doesn't even publish a difficulty/tier field, so the SwiftUI/SpriteKit layers can't observe a tier change to react to it | Absent, and currently un-buildable without a plumbing change first |
| Heat critical | Continuous vignette/shake/color escalation past 50%/72% thresholds | Well-built as a gradient, but no discrete "you just crossed into CRITICAL" moment |
| Game over / meltdown | Staged implosion → detonation → ~450 particles, fireball, lens flare, 3 staggered secondary pops, slow-motion dip, heavy+error haptics | The most "juiced" moment in the game — and it's entirely silent |

**Audio is completely absent.** A repo-wide grep for `AVFoundation`, `AVAudioPlayer`, `CHHapticEngine`, `SKAction.playSoundFileNamed`, and any `.mp3`/`.wav`/`.caf`/`.m4a` asset turned up **zero matches**. Every feedback moment in the game — tap, correct, wrong, combo, tier-up, meltdown — is silent. This is the single largest game-feel gap, larger than any individual finding above.

### Near-miss & momentum design

The engine already has one mechanic explicitly built to reward risk-taking: `heatRiskBonus` gives +35% score for solving at heat ≥ 75 ([GameEngine.swift:29-30,140](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L29-L30)). But it's **entirely unsold** — `speedBonus`/`chainBonus`/`heatRiskBonus` are computed on every `ReactionResult` and then silently dropped at the presentation layer; `ReactorGameScene.handleReaction` plays the identical VFX/haptic regardless of which bonuses fired. A player who nails a heat-risk save at 99 heat gets the same particle burst as one who idly solves at heat 5. There's also no "clutch save" texture: heat relief is a flat 12 regardless of how close to 100 you were, so digging out of near-meltdown isn't mechanically easier or more rewarding than an ordinary save beyond the score multiplier — no risk/reward lever the player can consciously pull. And the meltdown screen ([GameOverView.swift:13-14,62-86](NumReactor/GameOverView.swift#L13-L14)) shows only final score — no max combo reached, no "N points from your best," nothing that reframes a near-loss as "almost." `GameState` already tracks `bestScore` and `maxCombo`; neither is threaded into `ContainmentOverlay`.

### Onboarding

The *entire* instructional surface of the game is one static caption on the main menu — 12pt, opacity 0.35 — with no tutorial, coach-mark, first-run flag, or gated first reaction anywhere in the codebase (grep for `tutorial|onboard|firstRun|hint` returns nothing). To its credit, the phase-1 difficulty tier is genuinely gentler (fixed 2-stone sums, slowest heat gain, longest combo-decay grace, and a 20%-chance trivial single-stone target for the first 3 reactions) — but none of that tuning is surfaced to the player; heat starts draining the instant the screen appears, with no beat to just read the board first.

### Concrete, implementable suggestions

1. **Ship a minimal `SoundManager`** wired into the 5 spots that already have haptic calls (`ReactorGameScene.swift:517, 834, 875, 956-957`) — tap, correct, explosion. `SKAction.playSoundFileNamed` requires zero new dependencies and the file-system-synchronized group means dropping a `SoundManager.swift` + a `Sounds/` folder in `NumReactor/` needs no project file edits.
2. **Add a `UISelectionFeedbackGenerator().selectionChanged()` haptic on every tap/deselect**, not gated on the particle-burst condition — one line, [ReactorGameScene.swift:515-523](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L515-L523).
3. **Give overshoot a real signal.** Change `applyOvershootPenaltyIfNeeded` to return an optional result, thread it through `GameState` as a sibling to `lastReaction`, and consume it in `ReactorGameScene.update` for a red flash + warning haptic. This also closes the "silent wrong answer" gap flagged for onboarding.
4. **Consume the already-computed bonus flags.** `handleReaction` already receives the full `ReactionResult` — branch on `speedBonus`/`chainBonus`/`heatRiskBonus` to tag the floating score text ("+240 RISK") and scale shake/flash/haptic intensity. Zero new engine work; this is pure presentation using data that's already there.
5. **Differentiate combo break from combo increment** in `GameView`'s existing `onChange(of: gameState.combo)` handler — compare old vs. new value and branch to a shake/red-flash "deflate" on decrease instead of the same pop.
6. **Publish `difficulty`/tier through `GameState`** so a tier-up moment (ring flash + stinger) becomes buildable at all.
7. **Thread `bestScore`/`maxCombo` into `ContainmentOverlay`** for a "NEW BEST!" or "N points from your best" recap line.
8. **Bump the main-menu caption to ≥60% opacity and add a second line about heat** — trivial text change, closes half the onboarding gap for free.

---

## Phase 4 — New Game Mode Ideas

All 5 reuse the existing `GameEngine`/`DifficultyConfig`/`ReactorGameScene` machinery — none require new rendering primitives beyond a small HUD addition, and the already-built-but-unused `ContainmentOverlay(.stabilized)` win screen finally gets a use.

| Mode | Pitch | Why it's different | Effort | Reuses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Zen Sort** | Heat never rises; a fixed move counter shrinks per correct reaction instead, ending in a win (`.stabilized`) when it hits zero or a target count is cleared. | Removes time pressure entirely — shifts the skill from "react fast" to "pick the most efficient subset," a deliberate-planning loop instead of a reflex loop. | **Low** | `resolveIfCorrect`/`applyOvershootPenaltyIfNeeded`/`regenerateTarget`/`applyDecoys` untouched; renders the existing unused `.stabilized` win overlay. |
| **Blackout** | Stone values are masked (`?`) until tapped once to "scan" (costs a small heat tick), so players budget scans against the clock while building the sum from partial info. | Changes the core skill from arithmetic spotting to information-management-under-cost — a genuinely different interaction model (tap-to-reveal), not just harder arithmetic. | **Med** | `StoneKind` enum extended with a reveal state; `selectStone` entry point intercepts first tap; target/decoy generation untouched underneath. |
| **Overload** | Heat still climbs, but tapping the reactor core "cashes out" the current combo into banked score at a heat cost proportional to combo size; busting loses the whole unbanked run. | Introduces a genuine push-your-luck decision the base mode never has (base mode always immediately banks on every correct answer) — optimizes for *when to stop*, not how fast to solve. | **Med** | `combo`/`maxCombo`/`score`/`heat` fields directly; reuses the already-tappable reactor-core node and existing HUD. |
| **Isotope Match** | Each target carries a secondary constraint (even sum only / must include a decoy stone / no negatives), shown as a colored ring segment — a correct sum that violates the constraint is a miss. | The win condition becomes sum-match **and** a property of the chosen subset — a categorically different filtering skill, without touching pacing at all. | **Med** | `StoneKind`/`Stone.value`; `resolveIfCorrect` as the single gate to add a constraint check; `tryPickTarget`/`applyDecoys` unchanged. |
| **Meltdown Countdown** | Heat starts at 100 and drains passively while unsolved; every correct reaction pushes it back **up** — solving becomes risk, idling becomes safety, inverting the base polarity. | Flips what "success" means to the pressure meter, changing the optimization target from "solve fast to relieve heat" to "pace your solves against a countdown" — a genuinely different rhythm. | **Low** | `heat`, `tick()`'s integration, `heatGainPerSecond`/`heatReliefOnCorrect` — just flips sign behind a mode flag; all existing heat-driven visuals work unchanged since they're driven purely by the published `Double`. |

---

## Phase 5 — Prioritized Action Plan

Ranked by impact-to-effort ratio. **Items 1–5 are realistically shippable in the next update** — each is a small, contained diff against files you already own, with no new systems required.

1. **Fix the decoy jitter=0 bug** (Phase 2, Bug 3 / Phase 1, Critical #1) — exclude `0` from the jitter roll or add a post-clamp guard. One function, ~5 lines. Fixes a Critical, shipped-since-v1 correctness bug that quietly defeats the decoy system ~1-in-5 times it fires. [GameEngine.swift:287-303](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L287-L303)
2. **Close the zero-target free-reaction exploit** (Phase 2, Bug 2) — add the zero-check to the fallback path *and* require a non-empty selection in `resolveIfCorrect`'s guard. Two one-line defensive fixes. [GameEngine.swift:127,244-249](NumReactor/GameEngine.swift#L244-L249)
3. **Stop `lastReaction` from clobbering a real solve on rapid taps** (Phase 2, Bug 4) — `if let r = engine.selectStone(id: id) { lastReaction = r }`. One line; fixes a bug that silently drops feedback for a real win during the exact fast-tap play style the game's combo/heat system encourages. [GameState.swift:44](NumReactor/GameState.swift#L44)
4. **Add a tap haptic + a real overshoot/wrong-answer signal** (Phase 3, suggestions 2–3) — closes the single biggest "dead silence" gap in the moment-to-moment loop for a few lines of code, no new infrastructure. [ReactorGameScene.swift:515-523](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L515-L523)
5. **Ship a minimal `SoundManager`** wired to the 5 existing feedback call sites (tap/correct/explosion) — the highest perceived-quality jump available for a contained (few-hour) effort, since every hook point (particles, haptics) already exists; audio is the largest single game-feel deficit found. [ReactorGameScene.swift:517,834,875,956-957](NumReactor/ReactorGameScene.swift#L834)

**Next tier — worth scheduling soon after:**

6. Fix the ring-capacity shrink/grow bug before your most engaged players (the ones reaching phase 4, reaction 41+) hit visibly overlapping/untappable stones (Phase 2, Bug 1).
7. Consume the already-computed `speedBonus`/`chainBonus`/`heatRiskBonus` flags in `handleReaction` — pure presentation change against data you already compute (Phase 3, suggestion 4).
8. Differentiate combo-break from combo-increment in the HUD `onChange` handler (Phase 3, suggestion 5) — a few lines, fixes a loss currently reading as a gain.
9. Collapse the 3-way duplicated theme palette (`GameView`/`GameOverView` raw RGB literals + `HexChamberBackgroundView`'s reimplementation) into `ForgeTheme` `Color` accessors before the next palette tweak drifts them further apart (Phase 1, Should-fix #8).
10. Bump the main-menu instruction caption's contrast and add a line about heat (Phase 3, suggestion 8) — trivial, closes half the onboarding gap.
11. Enforce the particle `maxParticles` cap in the shared spawn helper instead of one internal path (Phase 1, Should-fix #10) — prevents overlapping bursts (rapid taps + death sequence) from spiking render cost.
12. Ship **Zen Sort** or **Meltdown Countdown** as a second mode (Phase 4) — both are Low effort and reuse the engine almost verbatim; either is a natural "new mode" App Store update headline.
13. Give `ReactorGameScene` a `reset()` that clears/rebuilds its own node tree instead of a full teardown+rebuild on every restart (Phase 1, Should-fix #9) — removes churn from the most frequently-hit path in the game.
14. Extract the explosion/meltdown choreography out of `ReactorGameScene` into its own type before building the (already-designed, currently-unused) win-condition celebration sequence — do this now rather than copy-pasting `detonate()` (Phase 1, Should-fix #6).
15. Thread `bestScore`/`maxCombo` into `ContainmentOverlay` for a "near-miss" recap on the meltdown screen (Phase 3, suggestion 7).
16. Publish `difficulty`/tier through `GameState` so a tier-up feedback moment becomes buildable (Phase 3, suggestion 6).
17. Centralize the retry-loop bounds, decoy jitter range, and 1.15s/0.15s retry-gate timing as named constants (Phase 1, Nice-to-have / Should-fix #7) — cheap insurance against the next person (possibly future-you) editing one half of a two-file contract.
